# Type 2 diabetes

> The cascade that opened with over-nutrition arrives here. When insulin-resistant, central fat outpaces
> what the pancreas can compensate for, blood glucose starts to rise — and diabetes is the point at which it
> stays high. Nutrition acts at *every* stage of that story: it can prevent the slide, manage established
> disease, and even put *early* diabetes into remission. The task here is to **recognise those levers** and
> **refer** for the detailed plan — safely, and without over-promising.

## Type 2 diabetes at a glance

This chapter follows directly from the *Obesity & Metabolic Syndrome* chapter: the insulin resistance that
defined that cluster is the soil type 2 diabetes grows in. It runs the length of the disease — **preventing**
progression in people with prediabetes, **managing** established diabetes through diet, and the newer story of
**remission** of early disease — and it hands the downstream complications forward to the *Cardiovascular
Disease & Hypertension* and *Chronic Kidney Disease* chapters. (Gestational diabetes — and why Singapore
screens every pregnancy — belongs to the *Pregnancy & Lactation* chapter, not here.)

Two commitments frame everything here:

- **Recognise and refer, don't prescribe.** You identify the nutritional levers — carbohydrate quality,
  a healthy pattern, weight loss for remission, cutting sugary drinks — then route to a **dietitian** for the
  individualised plan (medical nutrition therapy) and to the **diabetes team** for medication decisions.
- **Safety comes before any diet advice.** Before endorsing a low-carb diet or a fast, you must know which
  glucose-lowering drugs a patient takes — because some cause dangerous hypoglycaemia when carbohydrate is
  cut. That check is the spine of this chapter.

All of it sits inside Singapore's **War on Diabetes**, where roughly **one in three** people will develop
diabetes in their lifetime [@mohWarOnDiabetes].

## The science underneath: how insulin resistance becomes diabetes

Type 2 diabetes is not a switch that flips; it is the end of a long compensation. The mechanics of insulin
action and glucose handling belong to the *Integrative Metabolism* chapter, and the insulin resistance itself
to the *Obesity & Metabolic Syndrome* chapter — here we follow how they tip into disease.

![The diabetes continuum, and where nutrition acts — from at-risk, through prediabetes, to early and then established type 2 diabetes. Structured lifestyle change can prevent progression; a supervised, weight-centred programme can put *early* disease into remission; dietary quality manages established disease; medication (a specialist decision) is layered on, not a substitute.](/figures/mechanisms/t2d-continuum.svg){#fig-t2d-continuum width="92%" fig-align="center"}

**Compensation, then failure.** When tissues resist insulin, the pancreatic β-cells respond by making *more*
of it, and for years that keeps blood glucose normal. Diabetes appears when the β-cells can **no longer keep
up** — a progressive decline in insulin secretion on top of the resistance [@ada2026standards]. This is why
diabetes is usually preceded by a long, silent **prediabetes** phase, and why catching it early matters.

**The twin cycle.** A powerful way to understand *why* is the twin-cycle model: excess **fat inside the liver**
drives up fasting glucose, while fat inside the **pancreas** impairs the β-cells' ability to respond — each
feeding the other [@taylor2021remission; @taylor2025twincycle]. The important, hopeful corollary is that this
ectopic organ fat is *removable*: losing it — through substantial weight loss — can restore β-cell function
and **reverse early disease**, which is the basis of the remission story below. And because Asians carry more
fat at a given BMI (the *Obesity & Metabolic Syndrome* chapter's point), a person can cross their **personal
fat threshold** into diabetes at a lower weight than the Western cut-offs assume [@taylor2023pft].

## Carbohydrate: the main lever — quantity, but especially quality

Carbohydrate is the dietary component that most directly raises blood glucose after a meal, so it is the
natural focus. But the single most important message is a liberating one: **there is no mandated carbohydrate
target and no one "ideal" macronutrient ratio** for diabetes [@ada2026standards; @dyson2018diabetesuk]. What
matters is carbohydrate **quality** and **day-to-day consistency**, individualised by a dietitian — not a
number you set at the bedside.

**Quality beats quantity, and fibre is the clearest signal.** Choosing lower-glycaemic, wholegrain,
higher-fibre carbohydrates gives a small but real improvement in blood glucose (about 0.3% off HbA1c)
[@chiavaroli2021gigl] — modest, and best delivered as concrete swaps rather than by memorising glycaemic-index
tables, which are measured for single foods and blunted by mixed meals [@dnsg2023easd]. The strongest,
most consistent evidence is for **fibre and wholegrains**: higher intake lowers the risk of developing
diabetes and, in established disease, lowers HbA1c — with an optimum around **25–29 g of fibre a day**
[@reynolds2019carbquality; @reynolds2020fibrediabetes]. International guidelines converge on the same plate:
carbohydrate mainly from **wholegrains, vegetables, fruit and legumes**, minimising refined grains and sugary
drinks [@who2023carbohydrate].

![Swapping *toward* wholegrains — brown rice for white, wholemeal for white bread — is the highest-yield carbohydrate-quality move, and locally it is a swap, not a ban on rice.](/figures/food/brown-rice.jpg){#fig-brown-rice width="55%" fig-align="center"}

**Sugar-sweetened drinks are a discrete, high-yield target.** Each daily serving is associated with roughly a
**13–18% higher** risk of type 2 diabetes, and the effect is only partly explained by weight [@imamura2015ssb].
This is where Singapore's **Nutri-Grade** label earns its place as a decision aid [@shin2023nutrigrade], and
where the *kopi/teh* "**kosong**" (no sugar) or "**siu dai**" (less sweet) order is a genuine intervention.

::: {.callout-important}
## Substitution, not "rice is the enemy"
Pooled Asian data do link white rice to diabetes risk, but the **Singapore Chinese Health Study found rice
intake itself was *not* associated with diabetes** — while *substituting* wholemeal bread lowered risk
[@seah2019scriphealthrice; @hu2012whiterice]. The accurate, less stigmatising message is to shift *toward*
wholegrains (and add vegetables and protein to the plate), not to demonise a cultural staple. It matters
locally: only about **4% of the staples Singaporeans eat are wholegrain** [@mohNNS2022].
:::

## No single "best" diabetes diet — choose a quality pattern

Because quality beats the ratio, several **dietary patterns** are acceptable, and the right one is the one a
person will actually keep [@evert2019nutrition]. The **Mediterranean** pattern has the deepest diabetes-specific
evidence — lowering HbA1c in established disease and reducing new cases — and works well translated into local
food rather than adopted wholesale [@esposito2015medjourney; @koloverou2014medincidence]. A **low-carbohydrate**
pattern is a legitimate *option* with a real benefit at six months that tends to **attenuate by twelve** and
depends heavily on adherence — and strict versions need dietitian supervision [@goldenberg2021lowcarb]. A
**DASH-style** pattern also helps glucose, but its home — and its blood-pressure and lipid rationale — is the
*Cardiovascular Disease & Hypertension* chapter, so we signpost it here rather than re-teach it
[@fang2025dash4d]. (There are still too few diabetes-specific DASH trials to rank it confidently
[@jing2023networkma].)

The practical, low-numeracy tool that makes any of these work at a *cai png* stall is the **plate method** —
Singapore's **My Healthy Plate**: a quarter wholegrains, a quarter protein, half fruit and vegetables, with
water over sugary drinks [@hpbHealthyPlate].

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍜 Eating out
At the mixed-rice (*cai png*) stall the advice is concrete: **less rice (or brown rice), one lean protein, two
vegetables, go easy on the gravy**; at the drinks stall, **kopi/teh-o kosong or water** over a Nutri-Grade C/D
sweet drink. This *is* the Mediterranean/plate-method principle — more plants and wholegrains, less refined
carbohydrate and sugar — rendered in hawker-centre terms. (The Mediterranean pattern's *cardiovascular*
evidence, including PREDIMED, belongs to the *Cardiovascular Disease & Hypertension* chapter — here we use the
pattern for glucose and overall quality.)
:::

## Remission: reversing early diabetes

One of the biggest shifts in diabetes care is that **early type 2 diabetes can be put into remission** with
substantial, sustained weight loss. The landmark DiRECT trial achieved **46% remission at one year** with a
structured weight-management programme, versus 4% with usual care [@lean2018direct]. Three qualifiers
must travel with that number:

- **It is eligibility-bound.** DiRECT enrolled people diagnosed within about **6 years**, **not on insulin**,
  within a defined BMI range. Long-standing or insulin-treated diabetes behaves differently. Because Asians
  reach their fat threshold at a lower BMI, entry criteria should be **ethnicity-adjusted**, not the Western
  band applied unchanged [@taylor2023pft; @standby2022southasian].
- **It fades.** Remission fell to about **36% at two years and ~13% at five**, tracking with weight regained —
  the maintenance problem is not yet solved [@direct5yr2024].
- **It is *remission*, not *cure*.** The agreed definition is an HbA1c below the diabetes range **sustained off
  glucose-lowering drugs**, and monitoring continues because it can relapse [@riddle2021remission;
  @diabetesUK2021remission].

**How it is delivered matters for safety.** Remission programmes use a **supervised total diet replacement**
(a low-calorie formula phase with structured food reintroduction), and crucially the **medication is adjusted
by the clinician on day one**, because glucose and blood pressure fall fast. This is emphatically **not** a
self-directed crash diet — recognise the candidate and refer to a structured programme. Singapore has an
expert-consensus framework for this (with ethnicity-adjusted thresholds), though no national remission
programme yet exists [@khoo2026sgDSF].

## Preventing diabetes: structure beats a single conversation

Upstream of all this, prevention works — and the evidence is emphatic that **how** you deliver it decides
whether it works. In the Diabetes Prevention Program, an **intensive, structured lifestyle programme** cut
progression from prediabetes to diabetes by **58%**, outperforming metformin (31%) [@dpp2002], with benefit
that persists for decades even as it attenuates [@dppos2009; @dppos2025_21yr]. The active ingredient was the
*structure* — a multi-session curriculum with follow-up, aiming at ~5–7% weight loss and ≥150 minutes of
activity a week — not a one-off "you should lose weight." Translated at scale it still works: the NHS
programme and, locally, **Pre-DICTED** reproduced the benefit in real-world and multi-ethnic Asian settings
[@mcmanus2022nhsDpp; @bee2025predicted].

So the guideline advice for prediabetes is: aim for modest weight loss and activity, adopt a quality dietary
pattern, and **refer to a structured programme** [@ada2026prevention]. Frame it to motivate, not to frighten —
**not everyone with prediabetes progresses**, and some revert to normal [@hpbPreDictedInfo]. Sustaining any of
these changes is a skill in itself — the motivational-interviewing and behaviour-change toolkit is the
*Behaviour-Change Counselling* chapter's.

## Safety first: hypoglycaemia and the drug-class rule

Here is the do-no-harm heart of the chapter. Cutting or skipping carbohydrate — or fasting — is **only safe
once you know the patient's medication**, because a few glucose-lowering drug classes can cause dangerous
**hypoglycaemia**. So the first move, every time, is to identify the drug class:

- **Hypoglycaemia risk:** **insulin, sulfonylureas, meglitinides**. A patient on any of these who suddenly cuts
  carbohydrate can go dangerously low — **refer to the diabetes team to review medication first**
  [@ada2026glycemic; @aceT2DM2023].
- **Low risk used alone:** **metformin, DPP-4 inhibitors, SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 receptor agonists**. Here a
  sensible carbohydrate-quality change is reasonable — still refer to a dietitian.

For someone on a **fixed insulin** dose, the counselling message is **carbohydrate consistency** (steady timing
and amount), not aggressive restriction. And a conscious hypo is treated with the **"15-15" rule** — about 15 g
of fast-acting glucose, recheck in 15 minutes — *not* chocolate or nuts, whose fat slows absorption
[@diabetesukHypo]. Older people on sulfonylureas or insulin are especially vulnerable [@ling2021elderlyhypo].

The interactive below drills exactly this reflex: pick a patient's medications and see whether a low-carb plan
is safe to endorse, or whether it needs the diabetes team first.

<div data-island="carb-safety-check" data-src="/diagrams/carb-safety-check.yml"></div>

::: {.callout-warning}
## Check the drugs before you cut the carbs
It is genuinely unsafe to endorse a low-carbohydrate diet or a fast to a patient on insulin or a sulfonylurea
without a medication review — the dose set for their old intake can now drive them too low. Recognise the risk,
and refer to the diabetes team **before** the diet changes, not after.
:::

## Fasting for Ramadan with diabetes

For Singapore's Malay-Muslim community — which also carries a high diabetes burden — **Ramadan fasting** is a
high-yield, faith-aware counselling task, not a footnote. Most people with diabetes fast, and doing so on
insulin or a sulfonylurea raises the risk of severe hypoglycaemia several-fold [@salti2004epidiar]. Two things
are worth knowing and saying:

- **A finger-prick glucose test does *not* break the fast**, and Islam **exempts the medically unfit** — so
  monitoring and, where needed, not fasting are religiously permissible. Many patients wrongly believe
  otherwise, and some come to harm because of it [@zainudin2017knowledge].
- The safe approach is a **pre-Ramadan review** (ideally weeks ahead) to risk-stratify and adjust medication,
  following international guidance — a recognise-and-refer task, not one to manage single-handed
  [@hassanein2022ramadan]. Singapore has a locally validated fasting algorithm showing it can be done safely
  [@lum2020fast].

## The Singapore picture: the War on Diabetes

Singapore declared a **War on Diabetes** in 2016, and the framing runs through this chapter. About **one in
three** Singaporeans will develop diabetes in their lifetime; current prevalence is around **9%** of adults
[@mohWarOnDiabetes; @mohNPHS2024]. There is a real **ethnic gradient** — Indian and Malay residents carry
roughly twice the prevalence of Chinese residents — which should guide **screening priority**, framed as
population-level risk and never as blame; individual counselling stays individualised [@mohNPHS2024].

The local food environment is where the levers live: only ~4% of staples are wholegrain, and **sugary drinks
supply about half of dietary sugar** [@mohNNS2022]. The national tools point straight at these — **My Healthy
Plate**, **Nutri-Grade** on beverages [@mohNutriGradeFresh2023], and **Healthier SG** with its subsidised
screening as the delivery vehicle for early detection and lifestyle advice [@mohHealthierSG;
@mohHealthierSGScreening]. On medication, local **ACE guidance** names the classes (metformin first-line;
SGLT2 inhibitors or GLP-1 agonists where heart or kidney risk warrants) — which is exactly how to use it: to
know the tiers exist and that **prescribing is the team's job** [@aceT2DM2023].

## So — can diabetes be "reversed"?

Sometimes, and it is worth being precise about it. **Early** type 2 diabetes can be put into **remission** —
not cure — with a supervised, weight-centred programme that empties the liver and pancreas of ectopic fat; the
benefit is real but eligibility-bound and can fade. For most people, though, the everyday wins are upstream and
alongside: **prevent** progression with structured lifestyle change, **manage** with a quality carbohydrate
pattern and the plate method, and above all **counsel safely** — always checking the drug class before
endorsing a low-carb diet or a fast. Recognise the levers, refer for the individualised plan, and the diabetes
team and dietitian carry it from there. From here the cascade runs on — to the heart and blood vessels, and to
the kidneys.

## Check your understanding

<div data-island="quiz" data-src="/quizzes/type-2-diabetes.quiz.yml"></div>

## Practise: "Can I reverse my diabetes?"

Mr Raj is 50, diagnosed with type 2 diabetes two years ago, on metformin only (not insulin), with a BMI of 31.
He has read online that diabetes can be "reversed" and wants to know if it is true for him — and whether he
should start a very-low-calorie shake diet and stop his tablets himself. Work through the visit: check whether
remission is a realistic goal, get the *remission-not-cure* message right, and handle the moment where doing it
unsupervised would be unsafe. Your choices branch the case.

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/early-t2d-remission.case.yml"></div>
